content: Vaccines function by presenting the immune system with antigenic components of a pathogen—such as inactivated toxins, attenuated organisms, or recombinant proteins—thereby priming adaptive immunity without causing disease. Upon administration, antigen-presenting cells (APCs) internalize the vaccine antigens, processing and presenting peptide fragments on MHC molecules to naïve T cells within secondary lymphoid organs. This interaction, along with co-stimulatory signals, initiates clonal expansion and differentiation of antigen-specific CD4+ helper T cells and CD8+ cytotoxic T cells, depending on the vaccine type. Concurrently, B cells recognizing the antigen undergo activation, with T follicular helper cells facilitating germinal center reactions, somatic hypermutation, and affinity maturation, culminating in high-affinity memory B cells and long-lived plasma cells secreting neutralizing antibodies. This orchestrated response establishes immunological memory, enabling rapid and robust recall upon subsequent pathogen exposure. Additionally, vaccine adjuvants modulate innate immune signaling pathways, enhancing APC activation and shaping the quality and durability of adaptive immune responses.
